question: Can ROCm, Rust, and HIP materially speed up the current slow G1 residual-gate push?

followup32.assessment: A live but tiny descent was obtained. Most wall time is residual-only replay for finite-difference JVP columns and alpha candidates.

followup34.assessment: Wider local support increased runtime and did not descend, so repeated CPU support widening is not the right next acceleration path.

current_cached_jvp_speed_probe.scope: speed diagnostic only; this script uses the generic direct residual assembler and is not comparable to attached-policy residual closure

followup37_fastpath.assessment: The frame-force fastpath makes 480-column residual-only finite-difference correction cheap enough to keep exploring, but the accepted alpha remains tiny.

followup38_fastpath.assessment: The 600-column correction still descends, but the improvement tapered again; most remaining wall time is now setup and repeated candidate residual replay, not finite-difference matrix construction.

followup39_shell_bending_fastpath.assessment: A shell-bending/drilling-targeted pass still descended at roughly the followup38 rate, but the dominant hotspot stayed near the same shell/frame cancellation region.

followup40_shell_normal_fastpath.assessment: Targeting shell-normal rows produced only a 1.06e-6 N accepted descent, confirming that the current residual-row correction family is tapering too hard to reach the gate by repetition.

torch_rocm.assessment: Torch ROCm is installed but does not currently expose the RX 6900 XT as a cuda device, so Torch sparse/lstsq is not a usable acceleration path in this environment without runtime repair.

native_hip_smokes.assessment: Native HIP kernel execution is available and fast enough to justify a custom residual/JVP replay engine.

rust_engine_scope.current_scope: story-level nonlinear frame / hook and GPU Newton contract support
rust_engine_scope.assessment: Rust is useful as the stable FFI/orchestration layer, but the existing Rust path is not yet the full MGT frame-shell residual replay engine.

frame_internal_force.current_shape: Python loop over frame_elements with per-element 12x12 force recovery and scatter-add
frame_internal_force.acceleration_target: prepacked element arrays plus Rust/HIP element-parallel force recovery and deterministic scatter/reduction

shell_internal_force.current_shape: cached shell operator followed by CSR matvec K_shell @ u
shell_internal_force.acceleration_target: batched CSR matvec for multiple perturbed/candidate u vectors, or sparse target-row JVP extraction without full residual replay

row_correction_driver.current_shape: finite-difference support columns are evaluated sequentially through evaluate_row_candidate(), then alpha candidates are replayed sequentially
row_correction_driver.acceleration_target: batch all FD perturbations and alpha candidates through one residual replay engine and return only target rows plus residual inf metrics

validation.native_hip_frame_force_batch_assessment: The native HIP frame-force batch bridge now runs when the process has GPU device access. It provides a 129x kernel-level speedup over the CPU prepacked frame batch on followup40 with relative force error 7.1e-12. This is real but insufficient by itself because the latest residual frontier is dominated by shell bending/drilling and shell membrane rows.

implemented_followup.assessment: The first practical G1 replay acceleration is implemented for reference-geometry frame force recovery, and a CPU batch residual-only replay contract now validates frame/shell/spring candidate replay against the sequential evaluator. The native HIP frame-force batch bridge now runs with GPU device access and gives about 129x kernel-level speedup over CPU frame replay on followup40. The CPU batch path is only 1.16x faster for full residual replay, so the remaining high-impact backend work is a HIP/rocSPARSE shell CSR batch matvec behind the same residual replay contract.

advances.assessment: The fastpath made a 360-column element-block finite-difference row correction cheap enough to run, reopening descent beyond the prior support168 no-descent probe.
A second fastpath wide element-block pass still descended but with a smaller accepted alpha, indicating useful but tapering residual reduction.
Expanding the element-block search to 480 support columns still produced a gate-eligible descent, but the top hotspot stayed on node 2202 rx.
A 600-column follow-up remained numerically useful but tapered further, confirming that engine speed helps iteration throughput while the correction family still needs a stronger coupled direction.
Shell-bending/drilling targeting produced another small descent, but the improvement stayed near 5e-6 N and did not break the node 2202 shell/frame cancellation frontier.
Shell-normal targeting descended by only 1.06e-6 N, so the latest pattern is too slow for brute-force repeated CPU probes.

latest_frontier_pattern.assessment: The same shell-bending/shell-membrane/frame cancellation frontier remains active after followup40; the faster element-block lane helps exploration but has not closed the residual gate. Top-96 dominance remains shell_bending_drilling=39, shell_membrane=44, frame=13, so shell replay is the main acceleration target.

decision.expected_best_use: custom HIP/Rust residual replay and batched JVP engine, especially shell CSR batch matvec plus target-row residual metrics, not another sparse solver or Torch least-squares wrapper
decision.reason: The current accepted correction family is also algorithmically saturated. Engine throughput can shrink wall time, and native HIP frame replay is now proven fast, but the gate still needs a better coupled physical correction direction. Because the latest top residual rows are mostly shell bending/drilling and shell membrane, the next backend milestone is shell CSR batch replay on HIP/rocSPARSE rather than more frame-only acceleration.

rough_extrapolation_from_followup32.assessment: This extrapolation is optimistic because followup34 already showed no descent; it shows why algorithmic direction and replay throughput both matter.

rough_extrapolation_from_latest_fastpath.assessment: The latest shell-targeted passes show that brute-force repetition is now an obviously bad lane: more than ten thousand similar accepted probes would be needed. Backend acceleration is still useful, but only if it enables larger batched searches and stronger shell/frame coupled directions.

recommended_next_path: Do not spend the next G1 push on wider CPU finite-difference support sweeps.
Use the validated CPU batch residual replay contract as the boundary for a native Rust/HIP backend; CPU batching alone is only a modest acceleration.
Treat native HIP frame replay as proven for GPU-device executions: followup40 shows 129x kernel-level speedup over CPU frame batch with 7.1e-12 relative force error.
Implement shell CSR batch matvec behind the same batch contract and compare it against mgt_residual_batch_replay_followup38_probe.json and the latest followup40 frontier.
After the backend is faster than sequential replay, modify the row-correction driver to submit support-column perturbations and alpha candidates through the batch interface.
Use the faster backend to test larger shell-bending/membrane/frame coupled target sets, not to repeat the current tiny-alpha row correction thousands of times.
Keep the Rust layer as an orchestration/FFI boundary after the HIP shell/frame kernels are stable; the existing Rust crate is not yet the G1 full residual engine.
claim_boundary: This assessment proves engine availability and identifies the acceleration target. It does not claim G1 residual closure. HIP frame replay is validated only for frame internal-force batch replay; shell CSR batch replay and full residual gate closure remain open.
